coredata.dc:title: Label-free and ratiometric detection of nuclei acids based on graphene quantum dots utilizing cascade amplification by nicking endonuclease and catalytic G-quadruplex DNAzyme
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Guang-Li
Fang, Xin
Wu, Xiu-Ming
Hu, Xue-Lian
Li, Zai-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a ratiometric fluorescence assay based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the ultrasensitive DNA detection by coupling the nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling and the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme biocatalysis for cascade signal amplifications. With o-phenylenediamine acted as the substrate of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, whose oxidization product (that is, 2,3-diaminophenazine, DAP) quenched the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (at 460nm) obviously, accompanied with the emergence of a new emission of DAP (at 564nm). The ratiometric signal variations at the emission wavelengths of 564 and 460nm (I 564/I 460) were utilized for label-free, sensitive, and selective detection of target DNA. Utilizing the nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling and the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme biocatalysis for amplified cascade generation of DAP, the proposed bioassay exhibited high sensitivity toward target DNA with a detection limit of 30fM. The method also had additional advantages such as facile preparation and easy operation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence assay
G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of GQDs-based fluorescence method for the ratiometric, label-free detection of DNA
Analytical performance of the fluorescence assay for DNA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21575052 , 21275065 , 21005031 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( JUSRP51314B , JUSRP51513 ) and the Opening Foundation of the Public Health Centre at Jiangnan University ( JUPH201507 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis absorption (black line, left axis), excitation (red line, right axis) and emission spectrum (blue line, right axis) of the GQDs; (B) FT-IR spectra of the GQDs; (C) TEM images of GQDs. Inset image in A: Photographs of GQDs solution under a UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs (black line) and GQDs in the presence of OPD (red line) or DAP (blue line) produced by G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with the aid of 10mM H2O2, (B) UV–vis absorption, (C) Fluorescence spectrum of OPD (black line) and DAP (red line), and the excitation spectrum for DAP (blue line). Inset image in B: Photographs of OPD (left) and the DAP solution (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence decay curves of GQDs in the absence and presence of DAP.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the detection system to different concentrations of target DNA. From curve a to i, the concentrations of the target DNA are 0, 0.0008, 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, and 20nM respectively, (B) Plot of the fluorescence ratio (I
564 /I 460) of the detection system versus the logarithmical concentration of target DNA, four measurements for each point, (C) Fluorescence signals for 15nM of different DNA sequences: (T1) complementary sequence (target DNA), (T2) single-base-mismatched sequence, (T3) noncomplementary sequence. The error bars indicate the relative standard deviation of four repeated experiments.
Schematic illustration of the proposed bioassay for DNA detection.

head.ce:title: Label-free and ratiometric detection of nuclei acids based on graphene quantum dots utilizing cascade amplification by nicking endonuclease and catalytic G-quadruplex DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at:The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a ratiometric fluorescence assay based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the ultrasensitive DNA detection by coupling the nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling and the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme biocatalysis for cascade signal amplifications. With o-phenylenediamine acted as the substrate of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, whose oxidization product (that is, 2,3-diaminophenazine, DAP) quenched the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (at 460nm) obviously, accompanied with the emergence of a new emission of DAP (at 564nm). The ratiometric signal variations at the emission wavelengths of 564 and 460nm (I 564/I 460) were utilized for label-free, sensitive, and selective detection of target DNA. Utilizing the nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling and the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme biocatalysis for amplified cascade generation of DAP, the proposed bioassay exhibited high sensitivity toward target DNA with a detection limit of 30fM. The method also had additional advantages such as facile preparation and easy operation.

ce:section.ce:para: The integration of biomolecules with smart nanomaterials has enabled new strategies for bioanalysis. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a recently emerging member of fluorescent nanocarbon that containing graphene nanosheets with lateral dimensions less than 100nm, have attracted tremendous attention (Ponomarenko et al., 2008). Associated with quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs exhibit many special advantages such as large specific surface area, attractive biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness, efficient photostability and low toxicity (Ponomarenko et al., 2008; Shen et al., 2012; Lu et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2011). As a result, GQDs are becoming the most promising substitute for traditional semiconductor QDs as fluorescent luminophores. Up to now, the applications of GQDs for DNA detections are limited in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategies (Qian et al., 2014a, 2014b, 2014c). However, to conduct FRET detection, tough and time-consuming integrated work is inevitable to label the GQDs and another fluorescence agent (acted as fluorescence donor/acceptor of GQDs) with the DNA molecules. Though there are some label-free FRET strategies for DNA detection using fluorescent dyes (Zhou et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2014), the design of label-free approaches for the sensitive and selective detection of DNA based on GQDs is still challenging.

The amplified detection of DNA is motivated to achieve high detection sensitivity, which has great significance for a wide range of applications, such as clinical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, gene therapy, and sesing of genetic disorders (Cui et al., 2001; Eastman et al., 2006; Huang et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2010). Especially, the target DNA recycling method via nuclease is especially attractive for the detection of trace level of target DNA owing to its simplicity, rapidness and versatility for signal amplification (Zuo et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2013a). Nicking endonuclease (NEase) is a specific type of restriction endonuclease that recognizes specific nucleotide sequences in double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) and cleaves only one strand of the dsDNA at fixed position relative to the recognition sequence. After cleavage, the target DNA dissociates from dsDNA and then hybridizes to another probe DNA, inducing the next cycle of cleavage (Liu et al., 2014b). Thus, NEase provides target recycling signal amplification. In addition, DNAzymes (catalytic nucleic acids), especially the hemin intercalated G-quadruplex, introduce new catalytic tools for the development of amplified sensing assays when coupled with nuclease-assisted recycling for cascaded amplification (Wen et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2013). Similar to protein enzymes, DNAzymes exhibit high catalytic activities toward specific substrates, with other additional strengths, such as high chemical stability, cost effectiveness, synthesis facility, and broad substrate affinity (Wu et al., 2015). Usually, signal readout in these nuclease/DNAzymes couple detection systems merely rely on the absorption/luminescence of the being oxidized substrates. Although nanomaterials are potentially efficient fluorescence probes, they never integrate with nuclease and DNAzymes for the design of sensing platforms for DNA.

Taken together, herein, we developed a GQDs–based ratiometric, label-free and amplified fluorescence strategy for the detection of DNA by coupling the cascade amplification of NEase and DNAzyme. When a perfectly matched target DNA hybridized to the probe of hairpin DNA, the hairpin DNA was opened and a full recognition site would form for the NEase. NEase recognized a specific sequence on a double strand (dsDNA) and cut only one strand of dsDNA. In this case, NEase bound to and nicked the cleavage site, which triggered the target recycling and released the G-quadruplex DNAzyme sequences. After execution of the above strand-scission cycle, the released G-quadruplex DNAzyme sequences self-assembled with hemin to form G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for catalyzing the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to its oxidized product (2,3-diaminophenazine, DAP) with the aid of H2O2. The formed DAP quenched the emission of GQDs (at 460nm) obviously, accompanied with the emergence of a new emission of DAP (at 564nm). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 564/I 460) was utilized for the readout of the ratiometric assay. In contrast to the commonly used single intensity-based assay, ratiometric fluorescent probes allow the detection of target by the ratio of two distant fluorescent signals, thus exhibiting advantages of avoiding the influences of excitation intensity, probe concentration, and solution environments (Nolan and Lippard, 2007; Hou et al., 2014). As a matter of fact, the ratiometric fluorescence measurements are more reliable (Zhang et al., 2009; Fan et al., 2013). Due to the NEase-assisted target recycling and the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme-based biocatalysis for cascade amplification, this proposed bioassay exhibited a high sensitivity toward target DNA. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first GQD-based ratiometric fluorescence assay for DNA. Also, this method possesses many other distinct advantages, such as, label-free, reliable, and highly sensitive, which may become a powerful tool for nucleic acids detection in bioanalysis and disease diagnostics.
All oligonucleotides were HPLC-Purified and purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences of oligonucleotides are listed as follows:
Hairpin DNA: 5'-AAAGGGTTGGGCGG GATGGGTT GGATCAGTG↓CTTATT CGA AACCCATC-3'
(The italic bold letters are the sequence of the stem arms; the arrow indicates the nicking position).
Target DNA: 5’-TCGAATAAGCACTGATCC-3'
(The underlined letters are the recognition sequence of NEase).
One-base mismatch DNA: 5'- TCGAATAAGCACTGCTCC-3'
(The mismatched position is highlightened in italic).
Noncomplementary DNA: 5'- ATCGCCTTAACTGACATT-3'

The oligonucleotide stock solutions (100μM) were prepared in Tris–HCl buffer (100mM NaCl, 10mM KCl, and 0.002% (v/v) Triton X-100, pH 7.4) and diluted to the desired concentration with the Tris–HCl buffer. Cutsmart buffer solution and NEase (Nt.AlwI) were purchased from New England Biolabs, Inc (USA). Citric acid, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), hemin(III), H2O2(30%,w/w) and tri-hydroxymethyl aminomethane (Tris) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were purchased from Chengdu Organic Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). A 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared using NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4.

Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Co. Ltd., USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd., China). FT-IR spectra were obtained on an ABB Bomem FTLA 2000-104 spectrometer (ABB Bomem, Canada). Lifetime measurements were performed with a photoncounting spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS920, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

The GQDs were prepared by a pyrolysis method according to the literature (Dong et al., 2012).Typically, 2g citric acid was put into a 10mL beaker and heated to 200°C with a heating mantle for about 20min until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, 100mL of 10mgmL− 1 NaOH solution was added drop by drop into the obtained orange liquid under vigorous stirring. Finally, the obtained GQDs solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH solution. The as prepared GQDs were stored at 4°C for future use.

The detailed experimental procedure for DNA detection was as follows: Firstly, 100μM of hairpin DNA stock solution (in 10mM Tris–HCl buffer containing 100mM NaCl, 10mM KCl, and 0.002% (v/v) Triton X-100, pH 7.4) was heated at 90°C for 10min and gradually cooled to room temperature to dissociate any intermolecular interaction. In a series of 1.5mL centrifuge tube, 20μL of hairpin probe (10μM) was reacted with 20μL of target DNA (with varying concentrations) at 37°C for 1h, then 10μL of NEase reaction buffer (0.5U/μL) was added and incubated at 37°C for 90min. After that, 50μL of hemin solution (5μM) was added into the above solution, and the mixtures were kept at 25°C for 1h to form the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Subsequently, 100μL of 70μg/mL pretreated SWCNTs (Gao and Li, 2013) (the detailed pretreatment process is shown in the supporting information) was added into the G-quadruplex/hemin solution and incubated for 30min to adsorb superfluous hemin in solution. At last, the mixtures were centrifugated (18,000g) for 15min to remove redundant free hemin.

To conduct the fluorescence measurements, 400µL of 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0), 100µL of 0.5mg/mL GQDs, 100µL of the above prepared G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme solution, 40µL of 2mM OPD and 100μL of H2O2 (10mM) solution were mixed. Then the mixture was diluted to 1.0mL with deionized water and incubated at 40°C for 20min before the fluorescence measurements.

In order to investigate the optical properties of the prepared GQDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic characterizations were made. As shown in Fig. 1A, GQDs exhibits a well-defined absorption band centered at 352nm possibly attributable to n–π* transition of C=O bonds (Eda et al., 2010), and the strong fluorescence emission peak lies at 460nm (under the optimal excitation wavelength of 362nm) with a Stokes shift of 98nm. Bright blue luminescence of GQDs visible to naked eye is observed under the excitation of the UV light at 365nm (Fig. 1A, inset). Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the luminescence quantum yield of GQDs was calculated to be about 8.8% based on the referenced method (Fan et al., 2014). The quantum yield of GQDs was basically consistent with the previous report (Dong et al., 2012). Although the exact fluorescence mechanism of GQDs still remains a lot of controversy, up to now, more and more researchers attribute the bright fluorescence of the GQDs to radioactive recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes produced from efficient photo induced charge separations in the QDs (Cao et al., 2013). GQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment have proven to be with good quantum yields due to its well passivated surface most likely by the incompletely carbonized citric acid (Dong et al., 2012).

FT-IR spectra were also acquired to characterize the functional groups on GQDs (Fig. 1B). The peak at 3415cm−1 is responsible for the stretching vibrations of O–H, while the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COO give rise to characteristic absorption peaks at 1590 and 1390cm−1. And the peak at 1115cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–OH. In addition, the absorption peaks at 2925 and 2855cm–1 indicate the stretching vibration of saturated C–H, demonstrating that the surface of GQDs is incompletely carbonized (Dong et al., 2012). The FT-IR spectra confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, or carbonyl functional groups on the surface of GQDs. The morphology of the obtained GQDs is judged from TEM image (Fig. 1C), which indicates that the diameters of the as-prepared GQDs range from 3 to 5nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of GQDs-based fluorescence method for the ratiometric, label-free detection of DNA
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, a novel and ratiometric fluorescence assay for the label-free detection of DNA was developed using the nicking enzyme–assisted target recycling and the subsequently generated G-quadruplex DNAzyme with peroxidase-like activity for cascade signal amplification (the principle of the strategy is illustrated in Scheme 1). The hairpin DNA probe utilized in our sensing system is composed of three domains. The G-riched DNAzyme sequence (pink region) is partially caged in the duplex structure of the stem by hybridization with the purple region, and the green region is designed to recognize the target DNA (Scheme 1A). Thus, the restricted G-rich segment is prevented from binding hemin to form G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme in the hairpin DNA. Once hybridization with a perfectly matched target DNA, the hairpin DNA is opened and a full recognition site appears for the NEase due to the formation of the duplex structure. Then, the NEase is introduced into the system to act on the cleavage site and specifically cleaves the hairpin probe into two fragments. When the duplex is completely consumed, the released target can immediately hybridize with a new hairpin DNA and a next cycle of cleavage starts. By this means, one target strand has the ability to open a number of hairpin probes, resulting in the release of a lot of G-riched DNAzyme sequences. Upon the addition of hemin, the G-riched DNAzyme sequences would intercalate with hemin to form the activated G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme (Scheme 1B). We employed UV–vis absorption to confirm the binding of hemin to G-quadruplex. As shown in Fig. S1, the separate hemin had a typical absorption centered at around 397nm which corresponded to the Soret band (Xue et al., 2012). Once incubated in the solution containing G-quadruplexe sequence produced with the assistance of NEase, a sharp hyperchromicity with a red shift from 397 to 404nm was observed for hemin, indicating the formation of G-quadruplex/hemin structure (Kong et al., 2010).

Reports have demonstrated that G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes possess peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of a variety of substrates such as 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) (Liu et al., 2014b), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine sulfate (TMB) (Zhang et al., 2011), OPD (Liu et al., 2014a) or luminol (Gao and Li, 2013) for signal amplification because the catalytic activity of hemin experiences significant enhancement when inserting into the G-quadruplex structure (Kong et al., 2010). In this study, we chose OPD, a typical chromogenic substrate of natural peroxidase (Hamilton et al., 1999), as the substrate of the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Because OPD itself hardly affected the fluorescence of GQDs, while its oxidized product produced by the catalyst of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes with the aid of H2O2 quenched the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (at 460nm) obviously, with the accompanied emergence of a new emission at 564nm ( Fig. 2A). On the contrary, the oxidation products of other chromogenic substrates of natural peroxidase such as ABTS or TMB had no such interaction with GQDs as that of OPD (data not shown). Since the individual H2O2 or G-quadruplex/hemin or the mixture of H2O2 and G-quadruplex/hemin showed little influence on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, we speculated that the oxidization product of OPD was an efficient quencher for GQDs. The oxidization product of OPD displayed yellow-orange color (Fig. 2B, inset) with characteristic absorption peak at 420nm (Fig. 2B), proving that G-quadruplex/hemin behaved like natural peroxidase for the catalyzed oxidization (with the aid of H2O2) of OPD to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (Equation) (Fornera and Walde, 2010). There was no fluorescence signal output for OPD (Fig. 2C), and the emission at 564nm was attributed to its oxidization product of DAP (Yang and Wang, 2011). The maximum excitation wavelength of DAP lied at 420nm. It was proved in our experiment that the presence of G-quadruplex/hemin gave rise to a stronger fluorescence ratio signal (I 564/I 460) than that of hemin alone (Fig. S2). Evidently, G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with enhanced catalytic ability than free hemin contributed to the enhanced signal of the proposed strategy.

On the basis of the above phenomenon, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy was established by monitoring the fluorescence ratio at the two different wavelengths (564 and 460nm). Compared to the commonly used one wavelength detection strategy, ratiometric detection is a potentially powerful tool for probing trace amounts of analytes with the advantage of sensitivity and accuracy, which can effectively rules out high background interference or false positive signals (Nolan and Lippard, 2007; Hou et al., 2014). The catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme played an important role in DAP induced quenching. The more the number of catalytic DNAzyme units, the stronger the fluorescence ratio of I 564/I 460 (Fig. 4B). That is, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 564/I 460 increased gradually with the increased number of catalytic DNAzyme units, based on which a ratiometric fluorescence bioassay for target DNA was developed.

Hemin was the cofactor of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, and usually excess of hemin was necessary to be added into the detection system to ensure the complete formation of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. However, superfluous hemin in solution also catalyzed the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2 in our detection system (Fig. S3A), which inevitably gave rise to a high background signal. Thus, the problem of high background was yet to be solved to further improve the sensitivity of the assay. Fortunately, recent report demonstrated that free hemin could efficiently undergo adsorption on SWCNTs, whereas the G-quadruplex/hemin did not (Gao and Li, 2013). In view of this, SWCNTs were used to decrease the background of the sensing platform. As illustrated in Scheme 1B, after the target recycling amplification process, G-quadruplex DNAzyme sequences (pink region) of the hairpin probe were released and accumulated to intercalate with hemin to form G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. The superfluous hemin, which did not embed in G-quadruplex structure, was easily removed through centrifugation due to its strong affinity to SWCNTs, thus reducing the background signal. As shown in Fig. S3A and B, after treatment with SWCNTs, the concentration of free hemin in solution decreased greatly and the contribution from individual hemin for the signal change reduced obviously. Thus, we can say that SWCNTs significantly reduced the background from free hemin in the detection system. Although it was reported that hemin-functionalized graphene oxide nanosheets can also mimic the peroxidase due to the existence of hemin on the graphene oxide surface (Guo et al., 2011), we ruled out the possibility of the formation of hemin-functionalized GQDs with peroxidase-like activity, because the free hemin was removed by SWCNTs prior to the addition of GQDs in the detection system.

Furthermore, we explored DAP induced fluorescence quenching mechanism in this work. It is well-known that several mechanisms lead to fluorescence quenching, such as collisional (Olley et al., 2011), ground-state complex formation (Bano et al., 2010), aggregation (Zhang et al., 2013a), energy transfer (Zhang and Hu, 2010) or electron transfer (Zhang et al., 2013b). All the quenching mechanisms can be summarized as either static or dynamic quenching. The former is ascribed to the formation of a nonfluorescent ground-state complex, whereas the latter occurs as a result of diffusive encounters between the fluorophore and a quencher during the lifetime of the excited state (Sun et al., 2014). Since the two quenching mechanisms can be readily distinguished by observing the change of fluorescence lifetime, fluorescence lifetime measurements of GQDs in the absence and presence of DAP were conducted ( Fig. 3). The decay curves fits very well to a single-exponential function with acceptable residuals, and the average lifetime is 1.80ns for free GQDs and 1.27ns for GQDs/DAP (Table S1). The fluorescence lifetimes change by 29% in the presence of DAP, meaning dynamic quenching controls over static quenching in the detection system (Lin et al., 2015). As a classic electron acceptor (Hu et al., 2014), DAP quenches the fluorescence intensity of GQDs effectively probably due to photoinduced electron transfer mechanism. In addition, the emissive peak of GQDs (460nm) is partially overlapped with the absorption peak of DAP (420nm), indicating that energy transfer also occurs between GQDs and DAP. Thus, the quenching effect of DAP for GQDs was resulted from electron transfer and energy transfer.

Several experimental factors including the solution pH, catalytic temperature and nicking/catalytic reaction time were investigated detailed to determine the optimal conditions for the proposed fluorescence assay. Solution pH played an important role in the sensing system (Fig. S4A), and the optimal result was obtained at pH 7.0. Considering that G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme as peroxidase-mimicking DNAzyme owns the optimal catalytical temperature, responses of the sensing system at temperatures from 25 to 55°C was examined (Fig. S4B). The results confirmed that the fluorescence probe performed best at 40°C. Fig. S4C exhibits the influence of nicking reaction time of NEase on the fluorescence signal. The fluorescence ratio increased with time going on and reached equilibrium after 90min. The reaction time of H2O2-mediated oxidation of OPD catalyzed by G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme was found at 20min (Fig. S4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence assay for DNA
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the ratiometric assay for DNA using NEase and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme assisted cascade signal amplification was investigated under the optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensities at 460nm decrease significantly, while the peaks at 564nm keep on increasing with the increased concentrations of target DNA. The peak ratio of I 564/I 460 increases linearly with the logarithmical concentration of target DNA over the range of 0.1pM to 20nM (Fig. 4B). Based on three times signal–to–noise ratio (S/N=3), a detection limit of 30fM is obtained. Furthermore, we made a comparison between this assay with other reported methods for the detection of target DNA (Table S2). Obviously, the detection limit is not only much lower than GQDs-based target DNA detection using fluorometry (Qian et al., 2014a, 2014b, 2014c), but also it is much lower than other measurement protocols (based on other nanomaterials) such as electrochemiluminescence (Lu et al., 2014), photoelectrochemistry (Zang et al., 2014), electrochemistry (Hua et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015), and colorimetry (Li et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2013b). Evidently, the proposed method has great advantages: Firstly, as a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility (Wu et al., 2014; Nurunnabi et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012) of GQDs can ensure ideal signal output, in addition to the fact that the benign GQDs need not any complex modification process to conjugate with DNA. Secondly, by combing the merits of NEase-assisted target recycling with the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme-based signal amplification, a highly sensitive label-free fluorescence method for amplified assay of target DNA is developed. The hairpin probe does not require any chemical modification for labeling. Thirdly, the use of OPD as the substrate of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme makes the successful establishment of ratiometric fluorescence assay by observing the fluorescence ratios of GQDs and DAP, which further improves the reliability of our method. In short, all the advantages make our method have promising application prospect.

The specificity of the above bioassay was investigated by detecting the fluorescence response to the same concentration of different DNA molecules including the completely complementary target DNA sequence (T1), the one-base mismatched DNA sequences (T2), and the non-complementary DNA sequences (T3), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C, the maximum fluorescence ratio is achieved after hybridization with T1. However, once hybridized with T2, the fluorescence ratio only experiences slightly increase, while the non-complementary DNA sequences T3 shows tiny change compared to the blank signal. These results demonstrate that our sensing system exhibits outstanding specificity to distinguish the single-base mismatched DNA target, which is profited from the employment of the stem-loop DNA probe as the detecting part.

In this work, we developed a GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence bioassay for ultrasensitive DNA detection by the combination of nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling and the biocatalysis of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for cascade signal amplification. The proposed method exhibited high sensitivity toward target DNA with the detection limit of 30fM, which was far more sensitive than most of the DNA assays reported so far. Considering the remarkable advantages inherited, such as operation convenience and high sensitivity, this method may pave a new way to use carbon nanomaterials combining cascade signal amplification strategy for sensitive detection of DNA for diagnostic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575052, 21275065, 21005031), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP51314B, JUSRP51513) and the Opening Foundation of the Public Health Centre at Jiangnan University (JUPH201507).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.02.038.
